question_html: Draw the labelled diagram of an AC generator. Briefly explain its working and obtain the expression for the emf produced in the coil.
question_text: Draw the labelled diagram of an AC generator. Briefly explain its working and obtain the expression for the emf produced in the coil.
explanation: AC generator: Construction: A coil of n turns called armature rotates between the magnetic poles by the means of an external agent. The ends of the coils are connected to an external circuit through the means of carbon brushes and slip rings. Principle of working: AC generator works on the principle of Faraday's Laws of electromagnetic induction. According to it, when there is change of magnetic flux through a conducting loop, an emf is induced across the ends of the loop. {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{d t}{/tex} Working: The strong magnetic field is created between the poles. The coil ABCD is rotated by external means in this field. As the coil rotates, the angle between the magnetic field and the coil changes which induces an alternating emf. The ends of the coil is connected to an external circuit by the means of carbon brushes (B 1 & B 2 ) and slip rings (R 1 & R 2 ). When the external circuit is closed, an alternating current flows through the coil. Expression for emf produced: B = The magnetic field produced by the magnet The coil is placed such that at t = 0, the angle between the surface area vector of the coil and the magnetic field is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . {tex}\omega{/tex} = Angular speed of the coil Angle between surface area vector of the coil and the magnetic field after time t is given by: {tex}\theta=\theta_0+\omega t{/tex} Magnetic flux through the coil is then: Or, {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\int B \cdot d A{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}} {/tex} = {tex}B A \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}B A \cos \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right){/tex} By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, magnitude of induced emf through a coil of n-turns is given by: {tex}e=n \frac{d \phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{d t}{/tex} Or, {tex}e=n \frac{d}{d t}\left[B A \cos \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon|=n B A \omega \sin \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right){/tex} hence, the expression for the emf produced in the coil is {tex}|\varepsilon|=n B A \omega \sin \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right){/tex}

solution_html:
AC generator:

Construction:
A coil of n turns called armature rotates between the magnetic poles by the means of an external agent. The ends of the coils are connected to an external circuit through the means of carbon brushes and slip rings.
Principle of working:
AC generator works on the principle of Faraday's Laws of electromagnetic induction. According to it, when there is change of magnetic flux through a conducting loop, an emf is induced across the ends of the loop.
{tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{d t}{/tex}
Working:
The strong magnetic field is created between the poles. The coil ABCD is rotated by external means in this field. As the coil rotates, the angle between the magnetic field and the coil changes which induces an alternating emf. The ends of the coil is connected to an external circuit by the means of carbon brushes (B1 & B2) and slip rings (R1 & R2). When the external circuit is closed, an alternating current flows through the coil.
Expression for emf produced:
B = The magnetic field produced by the magnet The coil is placed such that at t = 0, the angle between the surface area vector of the coil and the magnetic field is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
{tex}\omega{/tex}= Angular speed of the coil
Angle between surface area vector of the coil and the magnetic field after time t is given by:
{tex}\theta=\theta_0+\omega t{/tex}
Magnetic flux through the coil is then:
Or,
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex}  = {tex}\int B \cdot d A{/tex}
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}} {/tex} = {tex}B A \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}B A \cos \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right){/tex}
By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, magnitude of induced emf through a coil of n-turns is given by:
{tex}e=n \frac{d \phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{d t}{/tex}
Or, {tex}e=n \frac{d}{d t}\left[B A \cos \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon|=n B A \omega \sin \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right){/tex}
hence, the expression for the emf produced in the coil is {tex}|\varepsilon|=n B A \omega \sin \left(\theta_0+\omega t\right){/tex}

question_html: State Lenz's law. Give one example to illustrate this law. "The Lenz's law is a consequence of the principle of conservation of energy." Justify this statement.
question_text: State Lenz's law. Give one example to illustrate this law. "The Lenz's law is a consequence of the principle of conservation of energy." Justify this statement.
explanation: According to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current (caused by induced emf) is always such as to oppose the change causing it. {tex}\varepsilon = - k\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}{/tex} where k is a positive constant. The negative sign expresses Lenz's law. It means that the induced emf is such that, if the circuit is closed, the induced current opposes the change in flux. Example: When the north pole of a coil is brought near a closed coil, the direction of current induced in the coil is such as to oppose the approach of north pole. For this the nearer face of coil behaves as north pole. This necessitates an anticlockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side [fig. (a)] Similarly when north pole of the magnet is moved away from the coil, the direction of current in the coil will be such as to attract the magnet. For this the nearer face of coil behaves as south pole. This necessitates a clockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side (fig. b). Thus, in each case whenever there is a relative motion between a coil and the magnet, a force begins to act which opposes the relative motion. Therefore to maintain the relative motion, a mechanical work must be done. This work appears in the form of electric energy of coil. Thus Lenz’s law is based on conservation of energy.

question_html: Write Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. Express it mathematically. A conducting rod of length l, with one end pivoted, is rotated with a uniform angular speed of {tex}\omega{/tex} in a vertical plane, normal to a uniform magnetic field B. Deduce an expression for the emf induced in this rod.
question_text: Write Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. Express it mathematically. A conducting rod of length l, with one end pivoted, is rotated with a uniform angular speed of {tex}\omega{/tex} in a vertical plane, normal to a uniform magnetic field B. Deduce an expression for the emf induced in this rod.
explanation: According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit (or coil). Mathematically, {tex}E = - N\frac{{d{\phi _B}}}{{dt}}{/tex} where N is the number of turns in the circuit and {tex}{\phi _B}{/tex} is the magnetic flux linked with each turn. Suppose the conducting rod completes one revolution in time T. Then Change in flux {tex} = B \times Area\;swept = B \times \pi {l^2}{/tex} Induced emf {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Change in flux}}}}{{{\text{Time}}}}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon = \frac{{B \times \pi {l^2}}}{T}{/tex} But {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \varepsilon = \frac{{B \times \pi {l^2}}}{{\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }}} = \frac{1}{2}B{l^2}\omega{/tex}
solution: According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit (or coil). Mathematically, {tex}E = - N\frac{{d{\phi _B}}}{{dt}}{/tex} where N is the number of turns in the circuit and {tex}{\phi _B}{/tex} is the magnetic flux linked with each turn. Suppose the conducting rod completes one revolution in time T. Then Change in flux {tex} = B \times Area\;swept = B \times \pi {l^2}{/tex} Induced emf {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Change in flux}}}}{{{\text{Time}}}}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon = \frac{{B \times \pi {l^2}}}{T}{/tex} But {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \varepsilon = \frac{{B \times \pi {l^2}}}{{\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }}} = \frac{1}{2}B{l^2}\omega{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit (or coil). Mathematically,
{tex}E = - N\frac{{d{\phi _B}}}{{dt}}{/tex}
where N is the number of turns in the circuit and {tex}{\phi _B}{/tex} is the magnetic flux linked with each turn.
Suppose the conducting rod completes one revolution in time T. Then
Change in flux {tex} = B \times Area\;swept = B \times \pi {l^2}{/tex}
Induced emf {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Change in flux}}}}{{{\text{Time}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon = \frac{{B \times \pi {l^2}}}{T}{/tex}
But {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \varepsilon = \frac{{B \times \pi {l^2}}}{{\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }}} = \frac{1}{2}B{l^2}\omega{/tex}

question_html: Define 'self-inductance' of a coil. Derive an expression for self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length {tex}l{/tex}, having n turns per unit length.
question_text: Define 'self-inductance' of a coil. Derive an expression for self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length {tex}l{/tex} , having n turns per unit length.
explanation: Self inductance of a coil is the ratio of the flux linkage to the current flowing in the coil. Expression for self inductance of a long solenoid: The magnetic field due to current flowing in the solenoid, {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Total flux linked with the given solenoid {tex}N \phi_{B}=(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n I\right) {A}{/tex} {tex}N \phi_{B}=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l I{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{N \phi_{B}}{I}{/tex} {tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l{/tex}
solution: Self inductance of a coil is the ratio of the flux linkage to the current flowing in the coil. Expression for self inductance of a long solenoid: The magnetic field due to current flowing in the solenoid, {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Total flux linked with the given solenoid {tex}N \phi_{B}=(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n I\right) {A}{/tex} {tex}N \phi_{B}=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l I{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{N \phi_{B}}{I}{/tex} {tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l{/tex}
solution_html:
Self inductance of a coil is the ratio of the flux linkage to the current flowing in the coil.
Expression for self inductance of a long solenoid:
The magnetic field due to current flowing in the solenoid, {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
Total flux linked with the given solenoid
{tex}N \phi_{B}=(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n I\right) {A}{/tex}
{tex}N \phi_{B}=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l I{/tex}
Self inductance
{tex}L=\frac{N \phi_{B}}{I}{/tex}
{tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l{/tex}

question_text: A rod of length l is moved horizontally with a uniform velocity v in a direction perpendicular to its length through a region in which a uniform magnetic field is acting vertically downward. Derive the expression for the emf induced across the ends of the rod. How does one understand this motional emf by invoking the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of the conductor? Explain.
explanation: Suppose a rod of length 'l' moves with velocity v inward in the region having uniform magnetic field B. Initial magnetic flux enclosed in the rectangular space is {tex}\phi=|B| l x{/tex} As the rod moves with velocity {tex}-v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} Using Lenz's law, {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B l x)=B l\left(-\frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=B l v{/tex} Suppose any arbitrary charge 'q' in the conductor of length 'l' moving inward in the field as shown in figure, the change q also moves with velocity v in the magnetic field B. The Lorentz force on the charge 'q' is F = qvB and its direction is downwards. So, work done in moving the charge 'q' along the conductor of length l W = F.l W = qvBl Since emf is the work done per unit charge {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{W}{q}=B l v{/tex} This equation gives emf induced across the rod.

Initial magnetic flux enclosed in the rectangular space is {tex}\phi=|B| l x{/tex} As the rod moves with velocity {tex}-v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} Using Lenz's law, {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B l x)=B l\left(-\frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=B l v{/tex} Suppose any arbitrary charge 'q' in the conductor of length 'l' moving inward in the field as shown in figure, the change q also moves with velocity v in the magnetic field B. The Lorentz force on the charge 'q' is F = qvB and its direction is downwards. So, work done in moving the charge 'q' along the conductor of length l W = F.l W = qvBl Since emf is the work done per unit charge {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{W}{q}=B l v{/tex} This equation gives emf induced across the rod.
solution_html:
1. Suppose a rod of length 'l' moves with velocity v inward in the region having uniform magnetic field B.
    Initial magnetic flux enclosed in the rectangular space is {tex}\phi=|B| l x{/tex}
    As the rod moves with velocity {tex}-v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
    Using Lenz's law,
    {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B l x)=B l\left(-\frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=B l v{/tex}
  2. Suppose any arbitrary charge 'q' in the conductor of length 'l' moving inward in the field as shown in figure, the change q also moves with velocity v in the magnetic field B.
    The Lorentz force on the charge 'q' is F = qvB and its direction is downwards.
    So, work done in moving the charge 'q' along the conductor of length l
    W = F.l
    W = qvBl
    Since emf is the work done per unit charge
    {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{W}{q}=B l v{/tex}
    This equation gives emf induced across the rod.

question_html:
How is mutual inductance of a pair of coils affected when:

  1. separation between the coils is increased
  2. the number of turns of each coil is increased
  3. a thin iron sheet is placed between the two coils, other factors remaining the same.
question_text: How is mutual inductance of a pair of coils affected when: separation between the coils is increased the number of turns of each coil is increased a thin iron sheet is placed between the two coils, other factors remaining the same.
explanation: When separation between the coils is increased, magnetic flux linked with secondary coil decreases. Therefore, mutual inductance (M) of pair of coils decreases. When number of turns of each coil is increased, M increases because {tex}M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{l}{/tex} As {tex}M \propto {\mu _r}{/tex} therefore, mutual inductance will increase on placing a thin iron sheet between the two coils.
solution: When separation between the coils is increased, magnetic flux linked with secondary coil decreases. Therefore, mutual inductance (M) of pair of coils decreases. When number of turns of each coil is increased, M increases because {tex}M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{l}{/tex} As {tex}M \propto {\mu _r}{/tex} therefore, mutual inductance will increase on placing a thin iron sheet between the two coils.
solution_html:
1. When separation between the coils is increased, magnetic flux linked with secondary coil decreases. Therefore, mutual inductance (M) of pair of coils decreases.
  2. When number of turns of each coil is increased, M increases because
    {tex}M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{l}{/tex}
  3. As {tex}M \propto {\mu _r}{/tex} therefore, mutual inductance will increase on placing a thin iron sheet between the two coils.

  2. A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, then what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
question_text: Define self-inductance. Write its SI unit. A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm 2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, then what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
explanation: When the current in a coil is changed, a back emf is induced in the same coil. This phenomenon is called self-induction. And self-inductance is the magnetic flux linked with the coil when a unit current flows through it. If L is self-inductance of coil, then net magnetic flux, {tex}N \phi \propto I \Rightarrow N \phi = L I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{{N\phi }}{I}{/tex} The SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H). Mutual inductance of solenoid coil system, {tex}M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } A } { l }{/tex} Here, {tex}N _ { 1 } = 15 , N _ { 2 } = 1 , l = 1 \mathrm { cm } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}A = 2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac { 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \times 15 \times 1 \times 2\times 10 ^ { - 4 } } { 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9} \mathrm { H }{/tex} Induced emf in the loop, {tex}|e_2| = M \frac { d I } { d t }= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9 } ×\frac { ( 4 - 2 ) } { 0.1 }{/tex} {tex} = 120 \times 3.14 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times \frac{2}{{0.1}}{/tex} {tex}= 7.5 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { V } = 7.5 \mu \mathrm { V }{/tex}
solution: When the current in a coil is changed, a back emf is induced in the same coil. This phenomenon is called self-induction. And self-inductance is the magnetic flux linked with the coil when a unit current flows through it. If L is self-inductance of coil, then net magnetic flux, {tex}N \phi \propto I \Rightarrow N \phi = L I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{{N\phi }}{I}{/tex} The SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H). Mutual inductance of solenoid coil system, {tex}M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } A } { l }{/tex} Here, {tex}N _ { 1 } = 15 , N _ { 2 } = 1 , l = 1 \mathrm { cm } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}A = 2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac { 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \times 15 \times 1 \times 2\times 10 ^ { - 4 } } { 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9} \mathrm { H }{/tex} Induced emf in the loop, {tex}|e_2| = M \frac { d I } { d t }= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9 } ×\frac { ( 4 - 2 ) } { 0.1 }{/tex} {tex} = 120 \times 3.14 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times \frac{2}{{0.1}}{/tex} {tex}= 7.5 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { V } = 7.5 \mu \mathrm { V }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. When the current in a coil is changed, a back emf is induced in the same coil. This phenomenon is called self-induction. And self-inductance is the magnetic flux linked with the coil when a unit current flows through it. If L is self-inductance of coil, then net magnetic flux,
    {tex}N \phi \propto I \Rightarrow N \phi = L I{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{{N\phi }}{I}{/tex}

    The SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H). 

  2. Mutual inductance of solenoid coil system, {tex}M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } A } { l }{/tex}

    Here, {tex}N _ { 1 } = 15 , N _ { 2 } = 1 , l = 1 \mathrm { cm } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    {tex}A = 2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore M = \frac { 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \times 15 \times 1 \times 2\times 10 ^ { - 4 } } { 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9} \mathrm { H }{/tex}
    Induced emf in the loop,
    {tex}|e_2| = M \frac { d I } { d t }= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9 } ×\frac { ( 4 - 2 ) } { 0.1 }{/tex}
    {tex} = 120 \times 3.14 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times \frac{2}{{0.1}}{/tex}
    {tex}= 7.5 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { V } = 7.5 \mu \mathrm { V }{/tex}

Given,

    {tex}\varepsilon= Blv{/tex}

  2. A  square loop of side a carrying a current I2 is kept at distance x from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current I1 as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the resultant force acting on the loop.
question_text: Define mutual inductance and write its S.I. unit. A square loop of side a carrying a current I 2 is kept at distance x from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current I 1 as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the resultant force acting on the loop.
explanation: Mutual inductance : It is the property of the coils that enables it to oppose the changes in the current in another coil. Its S.I unit is Henry (H). Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it. F 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it F 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathbf{I}_{1} \mathbf{I}_{2} a}{(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{a})}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire) Net force F = F 1 - F 2 F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}_{2} a}{2 \pi} { }\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{I_{1} I_{2} a^{2}}{x(x+a)}{/tex}
solution: Mutual inductance : It is the property of the coils that enables it to oppose the changes in the current in another coil. Its S.I unit is Henry (H). Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it. F 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it F 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathbf{I}_{1} \mathbf{I}_{2} a}{(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{a})}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire) Net force F = F 1 - F 2 F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}_{2} a}{2 \pi} { }\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{I_{1} I_{2} a^{2}}{x(x+a)}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mutual inductance: It is the property of the coils that enables it to oppose the changes in the current in another coil. Its S.I unit is Henry (H).
  2. Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it.
    F1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire)
    Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it 
    F2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathbf{I}_{1} \mathbf{I}_{2} a}{(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{a})}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire)
    Net force F = F1 - F2
    F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}_{2} a}{2 \pi} { }\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex}
    F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{I_{1} I_{2} a^{2}}{x(x+a)}{/tex}

question_text: Figure shows a rectangular conducting loop PQRS in which arm RS of length I is movable. The loop is kept in a uniform magnetic field B directed downward perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The arm RS is moved with a uniform speed v. Deduce an expression for the emf induced across the arm RS the external force required to move the arm and the power dissipated as heat.
explanation: Let RS moves with speed v rightward and also RS is at distances x 1 and x 2 from PQ at instants t 1 and t 2 , respectively. Change in flux, {tex}d \phi = \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } = B l \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} magnetic flux, {tex}\phi = \vec {B}. \vec {A} = BA cos 0^0 = Blx{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow {\text{d}}\phi {\text{ = Bldx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = Bl\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Blv\quad \left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} If resistance of loop is R, then {tex}I = \frac { v B l } { R }{/tex} Magnetic force = {tex}BIl \;\text {sin } 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { v B l } { R } \right) B l = \frac { v B ^ { 2} l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} Now, External force must be equal to magnetic force {tex}\therefore {/tex} External force {tex}= \frac { v B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} As, {tex}P = {I^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{vBl}}{R}} \right)^2} \times R = \frac{{{v^2}{B^2}{l^2}}}{{{R^2}}} \times R{/tex} {tex}\therefore P = \frac { v ^ { 2 } B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
solution: Let RS moves with speed v rightward and also RS is at distances x 1 and x 2 from PQ at instants t 1 and t 2 , respectively. Change in flux, {tex}d \phi = \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } = B l \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} magnetic flux, {tex}\phi = \vec {B}. \vec {A} = BA cos 0^0 = Blx{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow {\text{d}}\phi {\text{ = Bldx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = Bl\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Blv\quad \left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} If resistance of loop is R, then {tex}I = \frac { v B l } { R }{/tex} Magnetic force = {tex}BIl \;\text {sin } 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { v B l } { R } \right) B l = \frac { v B ^ { 2} l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} Now, External force must be equal to magnetic force {tex}\therefore {/tex} External force {tex}= \frac { v B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} As, {tex}P = {I^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{vBl}}{R}} \right)^2} \times R = \frac{{{v^2}{B^2}{l^2}}}{{{R^2}}} \times R{/tex} {tex}\therefore P = \frac { v ^ { 2 } B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let RS moves with speed v rightward and also RS is at distances x1 and x2 from PQ at instants t1 and t2, respectively.
    Change in flux, {tex}d \phi = \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } = B l \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} magnetic flux, {tex}\phi = \vec {B}. \vec {A} = BA cos 0^0 = Blx{/tex}]
    {tex}\Rightarrow {\text{d}}\phi {\text{ = Bldx}}{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = Bl\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Blv\quad \left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex}

    If resistance of loop is R, then {tex}I = \frac { v B l } { R }{/tex}

  2. Magnetic force = {tex}BIl \;\text {sin } 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
    {tex}= \left( \frac { v B l } { R } \right) B l = \frac { v B ^ { 2} l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
    Now, External force must be equal to magnetic force
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} External force {tex}= \frac { v B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}

  3. As, {tex}P = {I^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{vBl}}{R}} \right)^2} \times R = \frac{{{v^2}{B^2}{l^2}}}{{{R^2}}} \times R{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore P = \frac { v ^ { 2 } B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}

question_html: In Figure, shows planar loops of different shapes moving out of or into a region of a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Determine the direction of induced current in each loop using Lenz’s law.
question_text: In Figure, shows planar loops of different shapes moving out of or into a region of a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Determine the direction of induced current in each loop using Lenz’s law.
explanation: The magnetic flux through the rectangular loop abcd increases, due to the motion of the loop into the region of a magnetic field According to Lenz's law, this increase in flux is opposed by the induced current. The induced current must flow along the path bcdab so that it opposes the increasing flux. Due to the outward motion, magnetic flux through the triangular loop abc decreases due to which the induced current flows along bacd, so as to oppose the change in flux. As the magnetic flux decreases due to motion of the irregularly shaped loop abcd out of the region of the magnetic field, the induced current flows along cdabc, so as to oppose change in flux. Note that there is no induced current as long as the loops are completely inside or outside the region of the magnetic field.

The induced current must flow along the path bcdab so that it opposes the increasing flux. Due to the outward motion, magnetic flux through the triangular loop abc decreases due to which the induced current flows along bacd, so as to oppose the change in flux. As the magnetic flux decreases due to motion of the irregularly shaped loop abcd out of the region of the magnetic field, the induced current flows along cdabc, so as to oppose change in flux. Note that there is no induced current as long as the loops are completely inside or outside the region of the magnetic field.

  2. Due to the outward motion, magnetic flux through the triangular loop abc decreases due to which the induced current flows along bacd, so as to oppose the change in flux.
  3. As the magnetic flux decreases due to motion of the irregularly shaped loop abcd out of the region of the magnetic field, the induced current flows along cdabc, so as to oppose change in flux. question_html: Define the term mutual inductance between the two coils. Obtain the expression for mutual inductance of a pair of long co-axial solenoids each of length l and radii r1 and r2 (r2 > > r1). The total number of turns in the two solenoids are N1 and N2 respectively.
question_text: Define the term mutual inductance between the two coils. Obtain the expression for mutual inductance of a pair of long co-axial solenoids each of length l and radii r 1 and r 2 (r 2 > > r 1 ). The total number of turns in the two solenoids are N 1 and N 2 respectively.
explanation: A coil B kept near another coil A has magnetic flux passing through it when kept near coil A. The ratio of magnetic flux through the coil B to the current in the coil A is called as mutual inductance of coils. or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil M 12 = {tex}\frac{\phi_{2}}{i}{/tex} Let S 1 ​ carries a current i. Magnetic field inside S 1 will be B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} i }{l}{/tex} Flux through each turn of S 2 ​ is {tex}\phi{/tex} = B {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi r_{2}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} \pi r_{2}^{2} i N_{2}} {l}{/tex} Flux through each turn of S 2 ​ is {tex}\phi_B{/tex} = {tex}\phi N_2{/tex} {tex}\phi_{B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} \pi r_{2}^{2} i N_{2}}{l}{/tex} M = {tex}\frac{\phi_{B}}{i}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} N_{2} \pi r_{2}^{2}}{l}{/tex}
solution: A coil B kept near another coil A has magnetic flux passing through it when kept near coil A. The ratio of magnetic flux through the coil B to the current in the coil A is called as mutual inductance of coils. or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil M 12 = {tex}\frac{\phi_{2}}{i}{/tex} Let S 1 ​ carries a current i. Magnetic field inside S 1 will be B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} i }{l}{/tex} Flux through each turn of S 2 ​ is {tex}\phi{/tex} = B {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi r_{2}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} \pi r_{2}^{2} i N_{2}} {l}{/tex} Flux through each turn of S 2 ​ is {tex}\phi_B{/tex} = {tex}\phi N_2{/tex} {tex}\phi_{B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} \pi r_{2}^{2} i N_{2}}{l}{/tex} M = {tex}\frac{\phi_{B}}{i}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} N_{2} \pi r_{2}^{2}}{l}{/tex}
solution_html:
A coil B kept near another coil A has magnetic flux passing through it when kept near coil A. The ratio of magnetic flux through the coil B to the current in the coil A is called as mutual inductance of coils. or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil
M12 = {tex}\frac{\phi_{2}}{i}{/tex}
Let S1​ carries a current i.
Magnetic field inside S1 will be B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} i }{l}{/tex}
Flux through each turn of S2​ is {tex}\phi{/tex} = B {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi r_{2}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} \pi r_{2}^{2} i N_{2}} {l}{/tex}
Flux through each turn of S2​ is {tex}\phi_B{/tex} = {tex}\phi N_2{/tex}
{tex}\phi_{B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} \pi r_{2}^{2} i N_{2}}{l}{/tex}
M =  {tex}\frac{\phi_{B}}{i}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o} N_{1} N_{2} \pi r_{2}^{2}}{l}{/tex}

question_text: Deduce an expression for the mutual inductance of two long coaxial solenoids but having different radii and different number of turns.
explanation: Let coaxial solenoid P is wound over an another solenoid. Let l = length of both solenoid r 1 and r 2 = radii of P and S {tex}A _ { 2 } = \pi r_ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} [area of secondary coil, S] Magnetic induction in solenoid, P {tex}B _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 0 } n _ { 1 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} where, n 1 = {tex}\frac{N_1}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total flux linked with solenoid S, {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi r_2^2} \right){N_2} = \left( {{\mu _0}{n_1}{I_1}} \right)\pi r_2^2{N_2}{/tex} {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \left( \mu _ { 0 } \frac { N _ { 1 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \right) \pi r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi I _ { 1 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex} ...(i) But {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = M I _ { 1 }{/tex} were, M = coefficient of mutual induction {tex}\Rightarrow M I _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \Rightarrow M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex}
solution: Let coaxial solenoid P is wound over an another solenoid. Let l = length of both solenoid r 1 and r 2 = radii of P and S {tex}A _ { 2 } = \pi r_ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} [area of secondary coil, S] Magnetic induction in solenoid, P {tex}B _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 0 } n _ { 1 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} where, n 1 = {tex}\frac{N_1}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total flux linked with solenoid S, {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi r_2^2} \right){N_2} = \left( {{\mu _0}{n_1}{I_1}} \right)\pi r_2^2{N_2}{/tex} {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \left( \mu _ { 0 } \frac { N _ { 1 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \right) \pi r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi I _ { 1 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex} ...(i) But {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = M I _ { 1 }{/tex} were, M = coefficient of mutual induction {tex}\Rightarrow M I _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \Rightarrow M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let coaxial solenoid P is wound over an another solenoid.

Let l = length of both solenoid
r1 and r2 = radii of P and S
{tex}A _ { 2 } = \pi r_ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} [area of secondary coil, S]
Magnetic induction in solenoid, P
{tex}B _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 0 } n _ { 1 } I _ { 1 }{/tex}
where, n1  =  {tex}\frac{N_1}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}Total flux linked with solenoid S,
{tex}\phi _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi r_2^2} \right){N_2} = \left( {{\mu _0}{n_1}{I_1}} \right)\pi r_2^2{N_2}{/tex}
{tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \left( \mu _ { 0 } \frac { N _ { 1 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \right) \pi r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi I _ { 1 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex} ...(i)
But {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = M I _ { 1 }{/tex}
were, M = coefficient of mutual induction
{tex}\Rightarrow M I _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \Rightarrow M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex}

question_html: Define the term self-inductance of a solenoid. Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in an inductor of self-inductance L to build up a current I through it.
question_text: Define the term self-inductance of a solenoid. Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in an inductor of self-inductance L to build up a current I through it.
explanation: Using formula, {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} If {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=1{/tex} A/s, then L = {tex}|-\varepsilon|{/tex} Self inductance of the coil is equal to the magnitude of induced emf produced in the coil itself when the current varies at rate 1 A/s. Expression for magnetic energy: When a time varying current flows through the coil, back emf {tex}(-\varepsilon){/tex} produces, which opposes the growth of the current flow. It means some work needs to be done against induced emf in establishing a current I. This work done will be stored as magnetic potential energy. For the current I at any instant, the rate of work done is {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=(-\varepsilon) I{/tex} Only for inductive effect of the coil {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d W}{d t}=L\left(\frac{d I}{d t}\right) I \quad \Rightarrow d W=L I d I{/tex} From work-energy theorem, dU = LIdI {tex}\therefore U=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
solution: Using formula, {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} If {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=1{/tex} A/s, then L = {tex}|-\varepsilon|{/tex} Self inductance of the coil is equal to the magnitude of induced emf produced in the coil itself when the current varies at rate 1 A/s. Expression for magnetic energy: When a time varying current flows through the coil, back emf {tex}(-\varepsilon){/tex} produces, which opposes the growth of the current flow. It means some work needs to be done against induced emf in establishing a current I. This work done will be stored as magnetic potential energy. For the current I at any instant, the rate of work done is {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=(-\varepsilon) I{/tex} Only for inductive effect of the coil {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d W}{d t}=L\left(\frac{d I}{d t}\right) I \quad \Rightarrow d W=L I d I{/tex} From work-energy theorem, dU = LIdI {tex}\therefore U=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Using formula, {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
If {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=1{/tex} A/s, then L = {tex}|-\varepsilon|{/tex}
Self inductance of the coil is equal to the magnitude of induced emf produced in the coil itself when the current varies at rate 1 A/s.
Expression for magnetic energy:

When a time varying current flows through the coil, back emf {tex}(-\varepsilon){/tex} produces, which opposes the growth of the current flow. It means some work needs to be done against induced emf in establishing a current I. This work done will be stored as magnetic potential energy.
For the current I at any instant, the rate of work done is
{tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=(-\varepsilon) I{/tex}
Only for inductive effect of the coil {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d W}{d t}=L\left(\frac{d I}{d t}\right) I \quad \Rightarrow d W=L I d I{/tex}
From work-energy theorem,
dU = LIdI
{tex}\therefore U=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}

question_text: Define self-inductance of a coil. Obtain the expression for the energy stored in an inductor L connected across a source of emf.
explanation: Self-inductance of a coil is the property of the coil in which it opposes the change of current flowing through it. Inductance is attained by a coil due to the self-induced emf produced in the coil itself by changing the current flowing through it. Self-induction of the long solenoid of inductance L, (A long solenoid is one which length is very large as compared to its cross-section area.) the magnetic field inside such a solenoid is constant at any point and given by B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex} Magnetic flux through each turn of solenoid {tex}\phi{/tex} = B {tex}\times{/tex} area of each turn {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} A total flux = flux {tex}\times{/tex} total number of turns {tex}N \phi{/tex} = {tex}N\left(\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \times A\right){/tex} ... (i) If L is the coefficient of inductance of solenoid {tex}N \phi{/tex} = LI ... (ii) from equation (i) and (ii) LI = {tex}N\left(\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \times A\right){/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex} ... (iii) The magnitude of emf is given by |e| or e = {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ... (iv) multiplying I to both sides eIdt = LIdI but I = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} Idt = dq Also work done (dW)= voltage {tex}\times{/tex} Charge(dq) or dW = e {tex}\times{/tex} dq = eIdt substituting the values in equation (iv) dW = LIdt By integrating both sides {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{w} d W{/tex} = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{I_{0}} L I d t{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}{/tex} this work done is in increasing the current flow through inductor is stored as potential energy (U) in the magnetic field of inductor U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}{/tex}

Inductance is attained by a coil due to the self-induced emf produced in the coil itself by changing the current flowing through it. (i) If L is the coefficient of inductance of solenoid {tex}N \phi{/tex} = LI ... (ii) from equation (i) and (ii) LI = {tex}N\left(\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \times A\right){/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex} ... (iii) The magnitude of emf is given by |e| or e = {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ... (iv) multiplying I to both sides eIdt = LIdI but I = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} Idt = dq Also work done (dW)= voltage {tex}\times{/tex} Charge(dq) or dW = e {tex}\times{/tex} dq = eIdt substituting the values in equation (iv) dW = LIdt By integrating both sides {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{w} d W{/tex} = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{I_{0}} L I d t{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}{/tex} this work done is in increasing the current flow through inductor is stored as potential energy (U) in the magnetic field of inductor U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Self-inductance of a coil is the property of the coil in which it opposes the change of current flowing through it. Inductance is attained by a coil due to the self-induced emf produced in the coil itself by changing the current flowing through it.
Self-induction of the long solenoid of inductance L, (A long solenoid is one which length is very large as compared to its cross-section area.) the magnetic field inside such a solenoid is constant at any point and given by
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex}
Magnetic flux through each turn of solenoid
{tex}\phi{/tex} = B {tex}\times{/tex} area of each turn
{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} A
total flux = flux {tex}\times{/tex} total number of turns
{tex}N \phi{/tex} = {tex}N\left(\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \times A\right){/tex} ... (i)
If L is the coefficient of inductance of solenoid
{tex}N \phi{/tex} = LI ... (ii)
from equation (i) and (ii)
LI = {tex}N\left(\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \times A\right){/tex}
L = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex} ... (iii)
The magnitude of emf is given by
|e| or e = {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ... (iv)
multiplying I to both sides
eIdt = LIdI
but I = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex}
Idt = dq
Also work done (dW)= voltage {tex}\times{/tex} Charge(dq)
or dW = e{tex}\times{/tex}dq = eIdt
substituting the values in equation (iv)
dW = LIdt
By integrating both sides
{tex}\int_\limits{0}^{w} d W{/tex} = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{I_{0}} L I d t{/tex}
W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}{/tex}
this work done is in increasing the current flow through inductor is stored as potential energy (U) in the magnetic field of inductor
U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I_{0}^{2}{/tex}

A coil of number of turns N, area A, is rotated at a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} , in a uniform magnetic field B, and connected to a resistor R.
Deduce expressions for: (i) maximum emf induced in the coil. (ii) power dissipation in the coil.
question_text: A coil of number of turns N, area A, is rotated at a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} , in a uniform magnetic field B, and connected to a resistor R. Deduce expressions for: (i) maximum emf induced in the coil. (ii) power dissipation in the coil.
explanation: Suppose initially the plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field B. When coil rotates with angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} , then after time t, the angle between magnetic field vector B and normal to plane of coil is {tex}\theta=\omega \mathrm{t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} At this instant magnetic flux linked with the coil {tex}\phi=\mathrm{BAcos} \omega \mathrm{t}{/tex} . If coil contains, N-turns, then emf induced in the coil. If coil contains, N-turns, then emf induced in the coil {tex}\varepsilon=-N \frac{d \phi}{d t}=-N \frac{d}{d t}(B A \cos \omega t){/tex} {tex}=+N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For maximum value of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} , sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum emf induced, {tex}\varepsilon_{\max }=N B A \omega{/tex} If R is resistance of coil, the current induced, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Instantaneous power dissipated, {tex}P=\varepsilon I=\varepsilon\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R}\right)=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{N^{2} B^{2} A^{2} \omega^{2} \sin ^{2} \omega t}{R}{/tex} but, {tex}\left(\sin ^{2} \omega t\right)_{a v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (Average power dissipated in a complete cycle is obtained by taking average value of sin 2 {tex}\omega{/tex} t over a complete cycle which is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Average power dissipated {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{N^{2} B^{2} A^{2} \omega^{2}}{2 R}{/tex}
solution: Suppose initially the plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field B. When coil rotates with angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} , then after time t, the angle between magnetic field vector B and normal to plane of coil is {tex}\theta=\omega \mathrm{t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} At this instant magnetic flux linked with the coil {tex}\phi=\mathrm{BAcos} \omega \mathrm{t}{/tex} . If coil contains, N-turns, then emf induced in the coil. If coil contains, N-turns, then emf induced in the coil {tex}\varepsilon=-N \frac{d \phi}{d t}=-N \frac{d}{d t}(B A \cos \omega t){/tex} {tex}=+N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For maximum value of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} , sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum emf induced, {tex}\varepsilon_{\max }=N B A \omega{/tex} If R is resistance of coil, the current induced, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Instantaneous power dissipated, {tex}P=\varepsilon I=\varepsilon\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R}\right)=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{N^{2} B^{2} A^{2} \omega^{2} \sin ^{2} \omega t}{R}{/tex} but, {tex}\left(\sin ^{2} \omega t\right)_{a v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (Average power dissipated in a complete cycle is obtained by taking average value of sin 2 {tex}\omega{/tex} t over a complete cycle which is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Average power dissipated {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{N^{2} B^{2} A^{2} \omega^{2}}{2 R}{/tex}

1. Suppose initially the plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field B. When coil rotates with angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex}, then after time t, the angle between magnetic field vector B and normal to plane of coil is

    {tex}\theta=\omega \mathrm{t}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} At this instant magnetic flux linked with the coil {tex}\phi=\mathrm{BAcos} \omega \mathrm{t}{/tex}. If coil contains, N-turns, then emf induced in the coil.
    If coil contains, N-turns, then emf induced in the coil
    {tex}\varepsilon=-N \frac{d \phi}{d t}=-N \frac{d}{d t}(B A \cos \omega t){/tex}
    {tex}=+N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} For maximum value of emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex},
    sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = 1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum emf induced, {tex}\varepsilon_{\max }=N B A \omega{/tex}
  2. If R is resistance of coil, the current induced, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Instantaneous power dissipated,
    {tex}P=\varepsilon I=\varepsilon\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{R}\right)=\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{R}{/tex}

question_html: Derive the expression for the magnetic energy stored in a solenoid in terms of magnetic field B, area A and length l of the solenoid carrying a steady current I. How does this magnetic energy per unit volume compare with the electrostatic energy density stored in a parallel plate capacitor?
question_text: Derive the expression for the magnetic energy stored in a solenoid in terms of magnetic field B, area A and length l of the solenoid carrying a steady current I. How does this magnetic energy per unit volume compare with the electrostatic energy density stored in a parallel plate capacitor?
explanation: Rate of work done, {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}|\varepsilon| i{/tex} dW = {tex}\left(L I \frac{d I}{d t}\right) d t{/tex} dW = LI dl Total amount of work done, {tex}\int d W=\int L I d I{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} For the solenoid: Inductance, L = {tex}\mu_{0} n^{2} A E{/tex} ; B = {tex}\mu_{0} n I{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = U B = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{B^{2} A l}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex} Magnetic energy per unit volume Electrostatic Energy Stored Per Unit Volume stored in parallel plate capacitor = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
solution: Rate of work done, {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}|\varepsilon| i{/tex} dW = {tex}\left(L I \frac{d I}{d t}\right) d t{/tex} dW = LI dl Total amount of work done, {tex}\int d W=\int L I d I{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} For the solenoid: Inductance, L = {tex}\mu_{0} n^{2} A E{/tex} ; B = {tex}\mu_{0} n I{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = U B = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{B^{2} A l}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex} Magnetic energy per unit volume Electrostatic Energy Stored Per Unit Volume stored in parallel plate capacitor = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Rate of work done,
{tex}\frac{d W}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}|\varepsilon| i{/tex}
dW = {tex}\left(L I \frac{d I}{d t}\right) d t{/tex}
dW = LI dl
Total amount of work done,
{tex}\int d W=\int L I d I{/tex}
W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
For the solenoid:
Inductance, L = {tex}\mu_{0} n^{2} A E{/tex}; B = {tex}\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} W = UB = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}\right)^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{B^{2} A l}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex}
Magnetic energy per unit volume
Electrostatic Energy Stored Per Unit Volume stored in parallel plate capacitor = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. A toroidal solenoid with an air core has an average radius of 0.15 m, area of cross section {tex}12 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}{/tex} and 1200 turns. Obtain the self inductance of the toroid. Ignore field variation across the cross section of the toroid.
  2. A second coil of 300 turns is wound closely on the toroid above. If the current in the primary coil is increased from zero to 2.0 A in 0.05 s, obtain the induced emf in the secondary coil.
question_text: A toroidal solenoid with an air core has an average radius of 0.15 m, area of cross section {tex}12 \times {10^{ - 4}}{m^2}{/tex} and 1200 turns. Obtain the self inductance of the toroid. Ignore field variation across the cross section of the toroid. A second coil of 300 turns is wound closely on the toroid above. If the current in the primary coil is increased from zero to 2.0 A in 0.05 s, obtain the induced emf in the secondary coil.
explanation: {tex}B = {\mu _0}{n_1}I = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{l} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex} Total magnetic flux, {tex}{\phi _B} = {N_1}BA = \frac{{{\mu _0}N_1^2IA}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex} But {tex}{\phi _B} = LI{/tex} {tex}\therefore L = \frac{{{\mu _0}N_1^2A}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex} Or {tex}L = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1200 \times 1200 \times 12 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{2\pi \times 0.15}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} = 2.3 mH {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{d}{{dt}}({\phi _2}){/tex} where {tex}{\phi _2}{/tex} is the total magnetic flux linked with the second coil. {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{d}{{dt}}({N_2}BA) = \frac{{d}}{{dt}}\left[ {{N_2}\frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{{2\pi r}}A} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{{2\pi r}}\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1200 \times 300 \times 12 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 2}}{{2\pi \times 0.15 \times 0.05}}{/tex} = 0.023 V
solution: {tex}B = {\mu _0}{n_1}I = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{l} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex} Total magnetic flux, {tex}{\phi _B} = {N_1}BA = \frac{{{\mu _0}N_1^2IA}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex} But {tex}{\phi _B} = LI{/tex} {tex}\therefore L = \frac{{{\mu _0}N_1^2A}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex} Or {tex}L = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1200 \times 1200 \times 12 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{2\pi \times 0.15}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} = 2.3 mH {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{d}{{dt}}({\phi _2}){/tex} where {tex}{\phi _2}{/tex} is the total magnetic flux linked with the second coil. {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{d}{{dt}}({N_2}BA) = \frac{{d}}{{dt}}\left[ {{N_2}\frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{{2\pi r}}A} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{{2\pi r}}\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1200 \times 300 \times 12 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 2}}{{2\pi \times 0.15 \times 0.05}}{/tex} = 0.023 V
solution_html:
1. {tex}B = {\mu _0}{n_1}I = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{l} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex}
    Total magnetic flux, {tex}{\phi _B} = {N_1}BA = \frac{{{\mu _0}N_1^2IA}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex}
    But {tex}{\phi _B} = LI{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore L = \frac{{{\mu _0}N_1^2A}}{{2\pi r}}{/tex}
    Or {tex}L = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1200 \times 1200 \times 12 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{2\pi \times 0.15}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 2.3 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} = 2.3 mH
  2. {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{d}{{dt}}({\phi _2}){/tex} where {tex}{\phi _2}{/tex} is the total magnetic flux linked with the second coil.
    {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{d}{{dt}}({N_2}BA) = \frac{{d}}{{dt}}\left[ {{N_2}\frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}I}}{{2\pi r}}A} \right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{{2\pi r}}\frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{/tex}
    {tex}\left| E \right| = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1200 \times 300 \times 12 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 2}}{{2\pi \times 0.15 \times 0.05}}{/tex} = 0.023 V

question_html:
1. A rectangular coil of N turns and area of cross-section A is rotated at a steady angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field. Obtain an expression for the emf induced in the coil at any instant of time.
  2. Two coplanar and concentric circular loops {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. The radii of {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are 1 cm and 100 cm respectively. Calculate the mutual inductance of the loops. (Take {tex}\pi^2=10{/tex})
question_text: A rectangular coil of N turns and area of cross-section A is rotated at a steady angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field. Obtain an expression for the emf induced in the coil at any instant of time. Two coplanar and concentric circular loops {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. The radii of {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are 1 cm and 100 cm respectively. Calculate the mutual inductance of the loops. (Take {tex}\pi^2=10{/tex} )
explanation: The flux at any instant t is {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta=\mathrm{NBA} \cos \omega t{/tex} From Faraday‟s law {tex} \varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t) {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon=-N B A \omega \sin \omega t {/tex} {tex} M =\frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2}{100 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex} {tex} =2 \times 10^{-10} {H} {/tex}
solution: The flux at any instant t is {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta=\mathrm{NBA} \cos \omega t{/tex} From Faraday‟s law {tex} \varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t) {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon=-N B A \omega \sin \omega t {/tex} {tex} M =\frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2}{100 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex} {tex} =2 \times 10^{-10} {H} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. The flux at any instant t is
    {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta=\mathrm{NBA} \cos \omega t{/tex}
    From Faraday‟s law
    {tex} \varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t} {/tex}
    {tex} =-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t) {/tex}
    {tex} \varepsilon=-N B A \omega \sin \omega t {/tex}
  2. {tex} M =\frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2}{100 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex}
    {tex} =2 \times 10^{-10} {H} {/tex}

question_text: What is meant by current sensitivity of a galvanometer ? Mention the factors on which it depends. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-V){/tex} by using a resistance R. The magnetic flux through a coil of resistance {tex}5 \Omega{/tex} increases with time as: {tex} \phi=\left(2.0 {t}^3+5.0 {t}^2+6.0 {t}\right) {mWb}{/tex} Find the magnitude of induced current through the coil at {tex}{t}=2 {~s}{/tex} .
explanation: Current sensitivity of galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current. {tex}\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex} Factors Number of turns in coil, Magnetic field intensity, Area of coil, Torsional Constant {tex} R=\frac{V}{I}-G {/tex} for (0-V) Range {tex} R_1=\frac{V}{2 I}-G{/tex} for {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} Range {tex} \frac{V}{I}=R+G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\left(\frac{R+G}{2}\right)-G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\frac{R-\vec{G}}{2} {/tex} {tex}\phi=\left(2.0 t^3+5.0 t^2+6.0 t\right) {mWb} {/tex} {tex} |\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~V} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} {~A}=10 {~mA} {/tex}
solution: Current sensitivity of galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current. {tex}\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex} Factors Number of turns in coil, Magnetic field intensity, Area of coil, Torsional Constant {tex} R=\frac{V}{I}-G {/tex} for (0-V) Range {tex} R_1=\frac{V}{2 I}-G{/tex} for {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} Range {tex} \frac{V}{I}=R+G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\left(\frac{R+G}{2}\right)-G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\frac{R-\vec{G}}{2} {/tex} {tex}\phi=\left(2.0 t^3+5.0 t^2+6.0 t\right) {mWb} {/tex} {tex} |\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~V} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} {~A}=10 {~mA} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Current sensitivity of galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current.
      {tex}\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex}
      Factors Number of turns in coil, Magnetic field intensity, Area of coil, Torsional Constant
    2. {tex} R=\frac{V}{I}-G {/tex} for (0-V) Range
      {tex} R_1=\frac{V}{2 I}-G{/tex} for {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} Range
      {tex} \frac{V}{I}=R+G {/tex}
      {tex} R_1=\left(\frac{R+G}{2}\right)-G {/tex}
      {tex} R_1=\frac{R-\vec{G}}{2} {/tex}
  2. {tex}\phi=\left(2.0 t^3+5.0 t^2+6.0 t\right) {mWb} {/tex}
    {tex} |\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~V} {/tex}
    {tex} {I}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R} {/tex}

question_html:
1. A closed-loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets?
  2. A closed-loop move normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop?
    1. when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates.
    2. when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop.
  3. A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region (Fig.) to a field-free region with a constant velocity v. In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops.
  4. Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by Figure.
question_text: A closed-loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets? A closed-loop move normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop? when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates. when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop. A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region (Fig.) to a field-free region with a constant velocity v . In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops. Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by Figure.
explanation: No. However strong the magnet may be, current can be induced only by changing the magnetic flux through the loop. Or, The electricity get generated due to the phenomenon of electromagnetic induction. This happens due to changing of magnetic force by changing the position of the magnet but in this case, the magnet is held stationary. So no current is generated. No current is induced in either case. Current can not be induced by changing the electric flux. The induced emf is expected to be constant only in the case of the rectangular loop. In the case of a circular loop, the rate of change of the area of the loop during its passage out of the field region is not constant, hence induced emf will vary accordingly. The polarity of plate ‘A’ will be positive with respect to plate ‘B’ in the capacitor.

question_html:
Draw a schematic sketch of an AC generator describing its basic elements. State briefly its working principle. Show a plot of variation of

  1. magnetic flux and
  2. alternating emf versus time generated by a loop of wire rotating in a magnetic field.
question_text: Draw a schematic sketch of an AC generator describing its basic elements. State briefly its working principle. Show a plot of variation of magnetic flux and alternating emf versus time generated by a loop of wire rotating in a magnetic field.
explanation: Principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil. The direction of current is given by Flemming's left hand rule. It remains same during the first half turn of the armature. During the second half revolution, the direction of current is reversed. If N is the number in the coil, f is the frequency of rotation. A is the area of the coil and B is the magnetic field idensity then induced emf, {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{NBA} \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{ft})=2 \pi \mathrm{NBAf} \sin 2 \pi \mathrm{ft}{/tex} Therefore, the emf produced is alternating in nature and the current is also alternating. Current produced in a.c. generator can not be measured by moving coil galvanometer, because average value of a.c. over full cycle is zero. Graph between magnetic flux and time, is shown below: The graph between alternating emf versus time, is shown below:

Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil. The direction of current is given by Flemming's left hand rule. It remains same during the first half turn of the armature. During the second half revolution, the direction of current is reversed. If N is the number in the coil, f is the frequency of rotation. A is the area of the coil and B is the magnetic field idensity then induced emf, {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{NBA} \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{ft})=2 \pi \mathrm{NBAf} \sin 2 \pi \mathrm{ft}{/tex} Therefore, the emf produced is alternating in nature and the current is also alternating. Principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction.

Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil. The direction of current is given by Flemming's left hand rule. It remains same during the first half turn of the armature. During the second half revolution, the direction of current is reversed.
If N is the number in the coil, f is the frequency of rotation. A is the area of the coil and B is the magnetic field idensity then induced emf,
{tex}\mathrm{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{NBA} \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{ft})=2 \pi \mathrm{NBAf} \sin 2 \pi \mathrm{ft}{/tex}
Therefore, the emf produced is alternating in nature and the current is also alternating.
Current produced in a.c. generator can not be measured by moving coil galvanometer, because average value of a.c. over full cycle is zero.

  2. A horizontal straight wire of length L extending from east to west is falling with speed v at right angles to the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field B.
    1. Write the expression for the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire.
    2. What is the direction of the emf?
    3. Which end of the wire is at the higher potential?
question_text: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. A horizontal straight wire of length L extending from east to west is falling with speed v at right angles to the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field B. Write the expression for the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire. What is the direction of the emf? Which end of the wire is at the higher potential?
explanation: First Law: Whenever a conductor is placed in a varying magnetic field, an EMF gets induced across the conductor (called as induced emf), and if the conductor is a closed circuit then induced current flows through it. Second Law: The magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of flux linkages with the coil. Regarding the direction of induced emf, Lenz law suggests that, when an emf is induced according to Faraday's law, the polarity (direction) of that induced emf is such that it opposes the cause of its production. Given: Length of wire = v Velocity with which it is falling = v Magnetic field = B H = B Cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Angle between v & B, {tex}\Phi=90^{\circ}{/tex} As the wire fall downwards due to this motion e - within the wire also moves downwards and feels a force due to Earth's magnetic field towards west {tex}\vec{F}=-e \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \Rightarrow-e v B{/tex} As electrons moves towards west end of the wire they apply opposite or repelling force to new incoming charge. As more and more electron's come, eventually we achieve an equilibrium situation where no more electrons can come towards west side end. At this particular situation. eE = -evB E = -vB V = -EL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = +vBL e.m.f. setup within in the wire. The direction of the e.m.f. will be the polarity of the rod, which is positive at the east end and negative at the west end. As the electrons get accumulated at the west end it would mean that the west end is at negative potential. Which implies east end is at high potential (non zero potential).

1. First Law: Whenever a conductor is placed in a varying magnetic field, an EMF gets induced across the conductor (called as induced emf), and if the conductor is a closed circuit then induced current flows through it.
    Second Law: The magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of flux linkages with the coil.
    Regarding the direction of induced emf, Lenz law suggests that, when an emf is induced according to Faraday's law, the polarity (direction) of that induced emf is such that it opposes the cause of its production.
  2. Given:
    Length of wire = v
    Velocity with which it is falling = v
    Magnetic field = BH = B Cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    Angle between v & B, {tex}\Phi=90^{\circ}{/tex}
    1. As the wire fall downwards due to this motion e- within the wire also moves downwards and feels a force due to Earth's magnetic field towards west
      {tex}\vec{F}=-e \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \Rightarrow-e v B{/tex}
      As electrons moves towards west end of the wire they apply opposite or repelling force to new incoming charge. As more and more electron's come, eventually we achieve an
      equilibrium situation where no more electrons can come towards west side end. At this particular situation.
      eE = -evB
      E = -vB
      V = -EL
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = +vBL e.m.f. setup within in the wire.
    2. The direction of the e.m.f. will be the polarity of the rod, which is positive at the east end and negative at the west end.
    3. As the electrons get accumulated at the west end it would mean that the west end is at negative potential. Which implies east end is at high potential (non zero potential).

question_html: Define the mutual inductance of a coil. On what factors it depends? Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius r1 and the other of large radius r2 such that r1 << r2 are placed co-axially with centers coinciding. Obtain the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
question_text: Define the mutual inductance of a coil. On what factors it depends? Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius r 1 and the other of large radius r 2 such that r 1 << r 2 are placed co-axially with centers coinciding. Obtain the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
explanation: Mutual inductance is the property of a pair of coils due to which an e.m.f. is induced in one of the coils, due to the change in the current in the other coil. If a current I 2 flows through the outer circular coil of radius r 2 , the magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be, B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex} As r 1 << r 2 , so field B 2 will be considered to be constant over the entire cross-sectional area of inner coil with radius r 1 Hence, magnetic flux linked with the smaller coil will be: {tex}\phi_1{/tex} = B 2 A 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}} \times \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex} From the definition: {tex}\phi_1{/tex} = M 12 I 2 Hence, mutual inductance will be, M 12 = {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{I_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex} But M 12 = M 21 = M {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mutual inductance, M = {tex}\oint E \cdot d l{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}

solution_html:
Mutual inductance is the property of a pair of coils due to which an e.m.f. is induced in one of the coils, due to the change in the current in the other coil.

If a current I2 flows through the outer circular coil of radius r2, the magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be,
B2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex}
As r1 << r2, so field B2 will be considered to be constant over the entire cross-sectional area of inner coil with radius r1 Hence, magnetic flux linked with the smaller coil will be:
{tex}\phi_1{/tex} = B2A1
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 r_{2}} \times \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex}
From the definition: {tex}\phi_1{/tex} = M12I2
Hence, mutual inductance will be,
M12 = {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{I_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r_{1}^{2}}{2 r_{2}}{/tex}
But M12 = M21 = M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mutual inductance, M = {tex}\oint E \cdot d l{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
